key_insight: The study shows that hybrid halide perovskite surface passivation can be modeled with a two-stage, data-driven atomistic workflow that combines broad pretraining on diverse structures with targeted fine-tuning for a specific passivating molecule.
engineering_relevance: This approach offers a practical route to simulate realistic perovskite–molecule interfaces and guide defect-passivation design for more stable, higher-performance optoelectronic materials.
method: Built a large open training set of diverse metal and hybrid halide perovskite structures (hyP-26).
Added a smaller, specialized dataset for an amino-silane molecule interacting with a perovskite surface.
Used multistep continuous fine-tuning to adapt a machine-learned interatomic potential to the interface problem.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports a curated open dataset plus a specialized passivation dataset and applies the trained model to study how surface interactions evolve as amino-silane coverage increases.
watch_item: Performance may depend strongly on the quality and representativeness of the specialized fine-tuning data, so transfer to other molecules or surface chemistries may require new training.
paper_title: Data-driven atomistic modelling of hybrid halide perovskite passivation
